FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, first annual curated release, 4524 participants aged 8-11 from 20 US study sites

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time (recreational TV/screen) only; no qualifying smartphone or social media variable (B5: TV-only)

FT_IC3.evidence: Multivariable linear mixed-effects models examining relations with movement behaviour variables

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple β coefficients reported: e.g., β=1.44 (95% CI 0.82–2.07, p<0.0001) for each additional recommendation met; β=4.25 (2.50–6.01, p<0.0001) for screen time recommendation only

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using ABCD baseline data with multivariable regression

FT_EC7_reason: Paper fails FT_IC2 (no qualifying smartphone/SM variable), so FT_EC7 assessment not applicable
notes: Study measures 24-hour movement behaviours including sleep, physical activity, and recreational screen time. Focus is on screen time adherence rather than smartphone or social media use specifically.

FT_IC1.evidence: Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (ABCD) Study from the Netherlands, not US ABCD

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying smartphone variables: personal cell phone use and cordless phone use (both reported as continuous exposure measures)

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear regression models assessing associations between each exposure variable separately and each cognitive function measure; models adjusted for potential confounding variables

FT_IC4.evidence: Regression coefficients and standard errors reported in text and supplementary tables; e.g., results for RF-EMF exposure associations with cognitive outcomes in main results section

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using Amsterdam ABCD cohort with linear regression models

FT_EC7_reason: Paper fails FT_IC1 (not US ABCD Study; Dutch ABCD from Netherlands); FT_EC7 not applicable
notes: Study examines RF-EMF exposure from mobile phone base stations, presence of indoor RF-EMF sources, and personal cell phone and cordless phone use in relation to cognitive function in 5-6 year old children. While qualifying variables are present, the study uses Dutch ABCD rather than US ABCD Study.

FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, analytic sample N=9475 children with complete baseline data from 11,878 participants at baseline, mean age 9.9 years, enrolled 2016 with annual follow-up

FT_IC2.evidence: Smartphone ownership age (age of first smartphone acquisition); this is a qualifying smartphone variable per binding rules

FT_IC3.evidence: Multivariable logistic regression model examining associations between sociodemographic predictors and early smartphone ownership (<10 years) as binary outcome; odds ratios calculated

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple OR and 95% CI reported in Table 2: e.g., Female OR=1.38 (95% CI 1.19–1.60); Black OR=2.78 (95% CI 2.07–3.64); Single parent OR=2.01 (95% CI 1.67–2.44); Parental education <Bachelor's OR=1.70 (95% CI 1.40–2.06)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis examining sociodemographic predictors of early smartphone ownership in US ABCD Study

FT_IC6.evidence: No evidence of duplicate; published 2026 in Pediatrics

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional logistic regression design (B15 carve-out applies: FT_EC7 does NOT apply when ANY cross-sectional analysis with demographics → SM is reported). Paper uses cross-sectional multivariable logistic regression, not longitudinal/prospective analysis.
notes: Study examines sociodemographic associations with early smartphone ownership in US adolescents using ABCD baseline data. Smartphone ownership age is modeled as binary outcome (early <10 years vs. later). Multivariable model includes sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parental marital status, and parental education as predictors.
